questions.question: What additional component does a pushdown automaton have compared to a finite automaton?
options: A second input tape
An unbounded stack that provides infinite memory in LIFO order
A random number generator
A lookup table for all possible input strings
questions.explanation: A PDA extends a finite automaton with a stack. The stack provides unlimited memory, but only the top is accessible at any time (LIFO). This extra power lets PDAs recognize languages like {aⁿbⁿ} that DFAs cannot.

questions.question: In the CFG-to-PDA construction, what happens when the PDA's stack top is a variable A?
options: The PDA rejects immediately
The PDA nondeterministically chooses a production A → α and replaces A with α on the stack
The PDA reads the next input symbol
The PDA pushes A back onto the stack
questions.explanation: The standard CFG→PDA construction uses nondeterminism: when the stack top is a variable, the PDA guesses which production rule to apply and replaces the variable with the rule's right-hand side on the stack.

questions.question: Why can nondeterministic PDAs recognize strictly more languages than deterministic PDAs?
options: DPDAs have fewer states
Some languages require the machine to 'guess' the correct path (e.g., the midpoint of a palindrome) with no way to decide deterministically
DPDAs cannot read input symbols
Nondeterministic PDAs are faster
questions.explanation: For languages like palindromes over {a,b}, the PDA must guess where the midpoint is to start matching. No deterministic strategy can always make the right choice, so DPDAs cannot recognize all CFLs.

questions.question: What does the equivalence of CFGs and PDAs establish?
options: That PDAs are more powerful than CFGs
That the class of languages generated by CFGs is exactly the class recognized by PDAs
That all CFLs are regular
That PDAs can recognize all recursively enumerable languages
questions.explanation: CFG↔PDA equivalence means a language is context-free if and only if some PDA recognizes it. This links the generative perspective (grammars) with the recognition perspective (machines), both capturing exactly the CFLs.

questions.question: What additional capability does a pushdown automaton have over a DFA?
options: It can read input in both directions
It has an unbounded stack for storing symbols
It can make nondeterministic choices
It has multiple tapes
questions.explanation: A pushdown automaton (PDA) extends a DFA with a stack — a last-in-first-out storage that can grow without bound. This extra memory allows it to recognize context-free languages like balanced parentheses by pushing on '(' and popping on ')'.
